answer:
Verdict: Postgres-backed queue. 50k/day ≈ 0.6 jobs/sec — trivial load. Postgres handles thousands/sec with `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`.

Why:
- Already in stack. No new infra, no new failure mode, no ops burden.
- Transactional enqueue: job commits atomically with business data. Kills whole class of bugs (ghost jobs, lost jobs) that Redis/SQS need outbox pattern to fix.
- Free introspection: query jobs with SQL, easy retries, dead-letter, audit.

Pick library, don't build:
- Ruby → Solid Queue or GoodJob
- Python → Procrastinate or Celery+Postgres
- Node → pg-boss / Graphile Worker
- Elixir → Oban
- Go → River

When Postgres wrong choice:
- Sustained >5–10k jobs/sec — then SQS/Redis.
- Queue load threatens primary DB — mitigate with separate Postgres instance first, not new tech.
- Need fan-out/pub-sub across many services — SQS/SNS better fit.

Redis/Sidekiq: fast but adds infra, jobs can vanish on Redis crash without careful config. SQS: durable, cheap, but no transactions with your DB, 15-min visibility quirks, worse local dev. Neither buys anything at your scale.

Caveat: emails + webhooks bursty? Even 10x spike (500k/day) still fine on Postgres.
